sentences.text: CREW: ?] OK.
This is tape number 3.
One day, my mother bought something on this market.
It was on a cardboard, printed, the Russian alphabet.
And she said, you go ahead-- apparently-- she said, go ahead, here is a knife.
Cut the alphabets along the lines, and maybe you will learn how to read.
And the stepdaughter of this old lady came one day, and she showed me the letters.
And she showed me how to put together the letters to make words.
And that's how I start to learn to read.
It was not-- I was 10, eight years old, eight, seven years old.
The reason I did not go to this school is obvious.
My mother was always outside.
Theft was a-- a-- a daily occurrence, so she preferred to keep us inside, you know, my brother.
And I start to read.
I did not go to school.
Then she came, and she-- she showed me how to write.
But there was no paper in Russia.
Even prescriptions, the doctors used to write on pieces of-- of pieces-- newspapers.
In between the lines, they used to write the prescriptions.
So horrible was the situation with paper.
And I picked up those pieces of paper and these little-- little pieces of pencils.
And I used to came-- come home-- I came-- I came home, and I start to write.
And that's how I learned.
Then this lady was-- the teacher-- was kind enough-- I was, I think, the only one who was permitted to be enrolled in a school library without being a student there.
And I start to borrow first children's books, then bigger books, then biology books.
And I start to read in Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The last book I remember I borrowed from this library was I read it in Russian.
[SIGHS] Meantime, my daddy got in touch with us.

He send us his first pictures, which we'll-- you'll see it-- we'll see later My mother, at this time, was already dealing not with the bazaar selling soups, but she start to go to the nearby villages buying watermelons.
She was a strong young lady.
And she used to keep it in a big sacks in-- on her shoulders.
She used to run in the morning hours to the railroad station and to sell those watermelons to the passing-by echelons with Russian soldiers, who used to go to the front.
And that's how we start to live like humans.
Apparently, one day, a Russian soldier did not have money to buy the watermelon or whatever.
So he gave her his used uniform.
She brought the uniform, and she sewed for me, for my brother, two outfits, which you will see it on a picture.
You saw it on a picture.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father was injured.
He was in a hospital for a half-year.
He came back in 1946-- I'm jumping the history-- came back in 1946, demobilized already completely.
Even though he could-- he could be transferred and-- and to work in Moscow.
But he decide to come back to-- to his family.
And apparently, my mother wrote him that I'm-- I'm learning by myself how to read and write, apparently.
And-- and he-- and he brought-- he brought-- I don't know how he got it-- a Polish children's textbook.
It was not for me.
He somehow found it, you know.
It was issued in Russia for those who wanted to learn Polish.
Because not only Jews left to-- went to Russia, because of the huge amount of Poles, Christians went to Russia.
And he brought with him this Polish book.
I could not read it.
I could not read it, but my mother previously showed me how to-- the letters in Polish, which [?
Latin ?] letters.
So I start to read.
My father helped me how to read.
So I start to learn, also, Polish-- to read and to-- to-- and to write Polish.
So my-- my father came from the army.
[SIGHS]
Yes.
No, no.
The wars-- the war was over even-- the war was over, officially, the 9th of May.
And I remember the celebration on the streets.
I remember women used to cry during the war-- I want my husband to come without legs, but let him come.
Without hands, but let him come.
And my mother prayed-- prayed to God.
She said, I know they would not let him-- let him home if he will be all with-- all limbs he will be.
But let him be without a hand, but let him come home.
During this time, we were sick in the typhus.
I'm-- I'm very chaotic in that.
We were sick in typhus.
When I was-- when we were living in this neighborhood of the thieves, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they moved in next door.
They moved in Jews, Russian Jews, from Kiev, I think.
He was a baker.
And they had good, because he was a baker.
He was employed as a baker.
He had two daughters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember that my mother got sick on typhus.
They took her to the hospital.
They shaved off her hair.
They took her to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was hungry.
And one-- one afternoon, they were cooking.
The daughters were cooking, hmm, cooking something with rice, very thick.
And since I remember my mother used to tell us, don't you ever steal.
But at this time I was so hungry, I put my three little fingers into the pot-- it was already cool-- and I ate what they cooked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when my mother returned from the hospital, came back from the hospital, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they told her that I stole food.
And I got punished the second time.

And since then, since until today, since then I would never touch anybody-- I-- I used to, much later even, when I used to come to somebody and nobody was in the apartment or their room, I never walked into the room.
It was-- it was build in in me subconsciously.
Not-- not-- not-- not to touch anybody else's-- somebody else's thing.
And then we went to the hospital.
We were sick on typhus.
But my mother-- nobody used to-- could come to my mother.
But when I-- we went to the hospital-- both of us, my brother and me on typhus-- my mother used to come every single day in afternoon.
She used to bring us some tea.
Tea was scarce-- I don't know where she got the tea from-- to bring us the tea so we can drink tea.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [? these ?] some pieces of life.
There was a Jewish family, a mother, who had-- was sick and tired of it.
She had two boys.
I never saw this woman.
I never saw these boys.
But that's what my mother said.
One day, a soldier, a Jewish soldier-- she was Jewish-- a Jewish soldier approached her, and he said, let's go, let's go, let's leave this place.
We'll go to my place where I live.
And she left those two children in the middle of the street on a pillow and left the children as orphans, and she escape.
This was horrible.
A horrible thing, you know.
I'm just comparing two mothers.
My mother and the other mother.
This was war.
This was all war.
And in 1946, in June of 1946, with the last echelon, my parents decide to go back to Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went to Poland, and we-- we were settled in the city named Zlotoryja in 1946.
It was opened.
My father start to work also as a waiter.
My mother didn't work.
She was housewife.
And I start to go to a Jewish school, a Yiddish school.
I remember it was one room.
It was a small-- it was not a big city, you [? know, ?] very small.
It was maybe-- may-- maybe 50 families.
Jewish families, refugees came to those west-- southwestern part of Poland taken from the Germans.
The Germans claim that that's a German soil.
The Poles claim that it's Polish soil-- old Polish soil.
In Poland, yes, after the war.
The first time I performed, 1947.
There is a picture there.
I was-- I was probably good in the beginning of reciting poetry, poems, I was, you know.
And it was the first performance.
I start to get acquainted much better with Jewish holidays, Jewish traditions, et cetera.
And then in 1948-- 1947, my brother-- my brother Joseph was born in [POLISH].
I don't know why in a different city, different place.
But apparently, I don't know why.
And then my father found a job in [?
Legnica, ?] which is approximately 50 miles from Zlotoryja.
In [?
Legnica, ?] he found a job.
We moved to [?
Legnica. ?] In [?
Legnica, ?] I start to go-- there was another Yiddish school.
I start to go there, the fifth grade.
And I finish this-- finish that, the whole elementary school.
Then I went to high school, finished Yiddish high school, and later on became a teacher in this very school.
OK.
I was involved-- since the beginning I was involved in drama.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
Legnica ?] was a bigger-- bigger city with a much bigger population.
Of course, you cannot compare the population.
If [? we are ?] talking about bigger population, we're speaking about 400, 500, 600 souls.
It's not like it was before the war, or even in the United States, the Jewish population lives in-- in an inner city.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
And please, I don't want to forget to say-- say how my father found out about his family and what we found out-- if we could find out about a family who perished in the Holocaust.
During the war, my daddy was in the army, in the Russian Army.
As I mentioned before, he fought the Germans [? on ?] the Stalingrad.
He was wounded in-- in the leg while he was throwing a grenade to a tank.
He was-- his stomach was-- was down, because he was carrying-- there was no transportation for the Russian Army at the beginning, specially in beginning of the war.
So the soldiers used to carry on their shoulders not only their rifle, but also they used to carry the-- the mine-throwers, you know.
I don't know how to say it right in-- in-- in English.
It's-- it's on wheels that you are putting some-- some, you know, the big, big bullets or whatever.
And you're hiding your head, and it shoots-- shoot out.
My daddy was-- my father was in this-- this unit.
And he was-- because it was so heavy, his stomach went down.
So since-- since he died, he had problems with his stomach, with ulcers, with everything from the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he never-- this I have to-- I have to say it, that even though in Russia a lot of Jews changed their names, my father never changed his name.
His name was always Szlomo in-- in Russia, in Poland.
In Russia, they also ask for not only first name and last name, but also patronymic, patronymic.
And since his father was Yankev, Yankev, he used to call himself Szlomo [?
Yankevevich ?] Szczupak.
Never changed-- never changed his Jewishness, never for-- for a second.
It was because of the war, because of the Russians learned what's happened to the Jewish population, what's happened everywhere Jewish population.
Perhaps this.
No.
But I remember anti-Semitic outbursts.
For example, if I wanted to go outside in Russia-- before even we found out my father and during when my father came, you know.
But I-- very often boys knew with that-- oh, oh, Jew, oh, Jew, you-- you-- you this [? kind of ?] SOB, you Jew.
And they-- once even I got hit by-- by-- by-- by a boy because I was Jewish.
My mother was kicked and beaten on a market once when she was selling the soup by a wounded Russian soldier.
And he kicked her and beat her because, he said, oh, because of you Jews, we have to fight the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
What his-- he was in a hospital because of his wounds and because of the stomach.
And a matter of fact, he was-- this is-- he was-- we did not know anything about him when he was wounded, and he was in a hospital.
And he was in a hospital in the south, in Kazakhstan, 120 kilometers from us, which is very close.
It's-- it's apparently 75, 80 kilometers-- miles from us.
And we did not know anything about each [? other-- ?] about our father.
I did not know anything.
We did not know anything about-- about his existence, if he's--
--alive or not.
When we arrived.
When we arrived, we found out that, from a 3 and 1/2 million population of Jews, only 70,000 survived, mostly-- mostly in Russia.
Some survived in hiding by Christians.
And this is a very-- story I would like to share with what happened to my grandmother and my aunt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From my father's side and mother's side, according to my parents, they were approximately 300-member family, a family of 300 members.
None survived.
My father, in 1947-- he did not want to go there.
But 1947, he decide to go to the places where he came from to find out.
And he came to the village [NON-ENGLISH], and he found out what happened to his sister and his mother.
You remember I talked about that she decide to return.
It-- in Polish it is called [POLISH] or [POLISH], I don't know.
I cannot-- it was-- he was a Christian, a leader of the village, a Pole.
His name was Niewiadomski, and I-- I wanted to mention his name.
He was like a sheriff in the [? house. ?] Before, when Germans entered Poland and start to approach those areas, he decide to rescue the Jews of this village.
There were nine Jews in this village, nine souls.
He prepared in a cellar, under the barn, a hiding place for those Jews.
And he said to them, apparently, during the day, don't you even say a word.
During the night, whisper if you want to communicate.
I don't know.
Nobody knows how long will the war last.
But I know it's a horrible time for the Jews, and I [? want-- ?] you know.
And of course, they [? were-- ?] thank you.
For no-- just for human reasons, for human reasons.
This sheriff-- I'll call him sheriff-- Sheriff Niewiadomski, had two kids, a son, adult, and a daughter, an adult.
The son was in the partisans, in the guerrilla war.
His son-- his son was in the nearby woods fighting the Germans.
His daughter cooperated with the Gestapo.
And the father, Niewiadomski, knew about her-- about-- about her.
It was winter, 1944.
It was exactly maybe few months before liberation by the Russians.
She said, I think I heard human voices, a human cough.
I'm going to the Gestapo, and I'm going to tell them to search if you have Jews here or whoever.
Well, he said, go ahead and tell them.
As soon as she took her horse-- horse to ride to the Gestapo, he took another horse, apparently, and ran to the woods to his son.
He came to the woods.
He told his son.
The son organized a group of military men, you know, the guerrillas, to rescue those few Jews from the barn.
But when they-- when they approached-- approached the-- the edge of the woods, they saw that the barn was surrounded by the Germans, and the barn-- the barn was in flames.
That's how my father found out how his mother and his sister died, in the flames.
I saw the first time in my life my-- my father crying.
The other member of the family, how he died, my-- my mother found out-- my grandfather from my mother's side.
He was a strong man.
He was fighting in the World War I.
He was a very strong man, tall, strong.
I remember him standing by the oven before the war and warming his hands, very poor.
He was a coachman as a trade.
And he was employed by-- apparently by the Germans to carry-- to build a house.
They're building or-- or-- or renovating a tall house.
I don't know what was the purpose.
And it was slippery.
It was autumn time.
And though not only him, but a lot of other Jews, men, were carrying on their shoulders bricks on the shoulders-- special mounted bricks.
And they had to walk on plywood, not on regular stairs or a ladder, but on plywood to the fourth floor.
And apparently, he slipped and fell down and died.
He was killed this way.
That's the rest of the family, how they died.
I don't know.
The only-- the only thing what-- I don't know if it's news or not.
But they found out that Hersz, my uncle from my mother's side, Hersz Boksenbaum, he apparently survived the war.
But we searched.
My mother, my father, we searched.
We couldn't find him.
We searched different places.
Maybe it was so, maybe it was not.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's-- that's about what I can say, what I can recall right now about the Holocaust of my family.
Yes.
In [?
Legnica, ?] my father-- my father was a waiter, waiter.
Then he was-- he-- he decide to change his profession.
The reasons-- I don't remember the reasons.
But he changed the profession.
He start to work in a winery, you know, in a winery he was working.
He became-- he was-- he was also involved in-- in unions, in union.
And he was a leader of the union.
He was-- he was taking care of workers.
If they had some complaints or something, they used to come to him.
And through him, they used to-- they used to solve problems they had, et cetera, et cetera.
He was-- he was elected four times.
The people of [?
Legnica ?] elected him four times and re-elected as a councilman to the city hall.
A year after he passed away-- he passed away in '60-- in '65, 1965.
A year after he passed away, people did not know about it.
He used to come-- they used to come to my house, to my mother's house, ask for him.
Because he was-- for advice.
He was-- it-- it-- I don't know I should be humble or not.
But he was very wise.
People used to come for him for advice, even though he was not an educated person.
He educated himself.
He had very few grades passed.
Same thing, my mother.
My mother, when she was a girl, she could only finish second grade.
And she very often used to go to school with a half of a potato and a piece of herring, because that's all what they could afford.
It was long before the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So poor they were.
And when-- when after the war, some anti-Semites used to say-- and even today-- oh, Jews, they all rich.
[SIGHS] I don't know what to answer.
Sometimes I know, sometimes I don't want to answer. 95% of Jews were very poor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother used to-- when she was 15, she-- bits and pieces.
When 15, she used to sing [INAUDIBLE].
And her sister-- my mother used to tell me her sister was singing much better than even than she did.
She used to go out when she was 12 or 13, and she used to-- it was not plumbing in this city, in this [NON-ENGLISH].
She used to go-- she was very strong woman.
Apparently, during the war it was shown that she was strong.
She was strong carrying those watermelons on her shoulders, strong.
She used to go and pump the water for people who need the water.
They used to give her a few pennies, because she helped them to pump the water.
OK.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- it's bits and piece-- pieces of my-- of my life, of my parent's life.
[SIGHS] Yes, please, yeah.
